---
title: "Funnels and Parameterization"
author: "Mitzi Morris"
institute: "Stan Development Team"
date: June 22, 2026
format:
  beamer:
    highlight-style: tango
    aspectratio: 169
    pdf-engine: pdflatex
    theme: metropolis
    include-in-header: preamble.tex
    navigation: horizontal
    dev: png
    colorlinks: true
execute:
  eval: false
---

# Multilevel Models

For group-level parameters $\theta_1,\ldots,\theta_J$, a hierarchical prior
shares information through a common location and scale.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_j \mid \mu, \sigma_\theta &\sim \textsf{normal}(\mu, \sigma_\theta),
    \qquad j = 1,\ldots,J \\
\sigma_\theta &\sim p(\sigma_\theta) \\
\theta_j &= \mu + \sigma_\theta z_j,
    \qquad z_j \sim \textsf{normal}(0,1)
\end{aligned}
$$

\vspace*{12pt}


\tightlist
* Neal's funnel isolates the geometry created when one scale controls many effects.
* Small $\sigma_\theta$ pulls group effects toward $\mu$; large $\sigma_\theta$ lets them differ.
* Parameterization choice: sample $\theta_j$ directly, or sample standardized $z_j$ and transform.


# Multilevel Models and Neal's Funnel

* Neal's Funnel: extreme example of a challenging hierarchical prior

\vspace*{-18pt}
$$
y \sim \textsf{normal}(0,3), \qquad
x_n \sim \textsf{normal}(0,\exp(y/2)), \quad n = 1,\ldots,9.
$$

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="44%"}
\includegraphics[width=0.95\linewidth]{img/funnel.png}


:::
::: {.column width="54%"}
\vspace{0.05in}

\tightlist
* Canonical example has 9 lower-level parameters $x_1,\ldots,x_9$.
* One population-scale parameter $y$ controls their prior scale.
* To explore neck of the funnel: small steps on x-axis, large steps on y-axis.
* To explore mouth: large steps on x-axis, small steps on y-axis.
:::
::::



# Funnel Example: Stan Implementations

\fontsize{9pt}{9.5}\selectfont

\begincols
\begincol{0.46\linewidth}
\textbf{Centered parameterization}

\vspace{0.08in}

```stan
parameters {
  real y;
  vector[9] x;
}
model {
  y ~ normal(0, 3);
  x ~ normal(0, exp(y / 2));
}
```
\endcol
\begincol{0.52\linewidth}
\textbf{Non-centered parameterization}

\vspace{0.08in}

```stan
parameters {
  real y_std;
  vector[9] x_std;
}
transformed parameters {
  real y = 3 * y_std;
  vector[9] x = exp(y / 2) * x_std;
}
model {
  y_std ~ std_normal();
  x_std ~ std_normal();
}
```
\endcol
\endcols
\normalsize

# Low-data Regime

\fontsize{10pt}{10.5}\selectfont

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
\textbf{Centered}

\tightlist
* Cannot explore neck of funnel.
* Many divergences.
* Low-data regime is prior-dominated.

\vspace{0.04in}
\includegraphics[width=0.75\linewidth]{img/funnel_fit_cp.png}
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
\textbf{Non-centered}

\tightlist
* Explores further.
* No divergences in this example.
* Works well when group effects are weakly identified.

\vspace{0.1in}
\includegraphics[width=0.78\linewidth]{img/funnel_fit_nc.png}
:::
::::
\normalsize

# What Happens as Data Grow?

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="62%"}
\includegraphics[width=0.98\linewidth]{img/cp_vs_ncp.png}
:::
::: {.column width="36%"}
\vspace{0.08in}

\tightlist
* 10 groups, intercept-only hierarchical logistic regression.
* Fit model with 0, 4, 16, 4096 observations.
* 20,000 draws; divergences in orange.
* Low data: non-centered avoids the funnel.
* More data: non-centered starts to warp.
:::
::::

# Varying Slope / Varying Intercept Model

Let $x_n$ include a column of ones for the group-level intercept and additional
columns for group-level predictors.

\vspace{-0.05in}

$$
\begin{aligned}
y_n &\sim \textsf{normal}(\eta_n, \sigma) \\
\eta_n &= \alpha + z_n \gamma + x_n^\top \beta_{g[n]} \\
\beta_j &\sim \operatorname{multi\_normal}(\mu_\beta, \Sigma_\beta)
\end{aligned}
$$

\tightlist
* $j = 1,\ldots,J$ indexes groups.
* $\beta_j$ is a length-$K$ vector: intercept, slope, and other group-level coefficients.
* $\Sigma_\beta = \mathrm{diag}(\tau)\,\Omega\,\mathrm{diag}(\tau)$.

# Varying Effects: Choose One Parameterization

Both versions define the same model-scale object used by the likelihood:
`array[J] vector[K] beta`.

\fontsize{7.7pt}{8.1}\selectfont
\begincols
\begincol{0.49\linewidth}
\textbf{Centered parameterization}

```stan
parameters {
  vector[K] mu_beta;
  vector<lower=0>[K] tau;
  cholesky_factor_corr[K] L_Omega;
  array[J] vector[K] beta;
}
model {
  matrix[K, K] L_Sigma =
    diag_pre_multiply(tau, L_Omega);

  mu_beta ~ normal(0, 1);
  tau ~ exponential(1);
  L_Omega ~ lkj_corr_cholesky(2);

  for (j in 1:J)
    beta[j] ~ multi_normal_cholesky(mu_beta,
                                    L_Sigma);
}
```
\endcol
\begincol{0.49\linewidth}
\textbf{Non-centered parameterization}

```stan
parameters {
  vector[K] mu_beta;
  vector<lower=0>[K] tau;
  cholesky_factor_corr[K] L_Omega;
  matrix[K, J] beta_std;
}
transformed parameters {
  array[J] vector[K] beta;
  {
    matrix[K, J] beta_mat =
      rep_matrix(mu_beta, J)
      + diag_pre_multiply(tau, L_Omega) * beta_std;
    for (j in 1:J) beta[j] = beta_mat[, j];
  }
}
model {
  mu_beta ~ normal(0, 1);
  tau ~ exponential(1);
  L_Omega ~ lkj_corr_cholesky(2);
  to_vector(beta_std) ~ std_normal();
}
```
\endcol
\endcols
\normalsize

# Common Likelihood After Either Choice

\fontsize{8.5pt}{9}\selectfont
```stan
data {
  int<lower=1> N;   int<lower=1> J;   int<lower=1> K;   int<lower=0> P;
  array[N] int<lower=1, upper=J> group;
  matrix[N, P] z_pop;        // population-level predictors
  matrix[N, K] x_grp;        // first column is 1 for varying intercept
  vector[N] y;
}
parameters {
  real alpha;   vector[P] gamma;   real<lower=0> sigma;
  // plus one varying-effects block: centered or non-centered
}
model {
  alpha ~ normal(0, 1);   gamma ~ normal(0, 1);   sigma ~ exponential(1);
  {
    vector[N] eta = rep_vector(alpha, N) + z_pop * gamma;
    for (n in 1:N) eta[n] += x_grp[n] * beta[group[n]];
    y ~ normal(eta, sigma);
  }
  // plus priors from the selected varying-effects block
}
```
\normalsize

# Same Model, Different Geometry

\tightlist
* Centered:
  + sample group coefficients directly from the population distribution.
  + often better when each group has many observations.

* Non-centered:
  + sample standardized group coefficients.
  + transform by population location, scale, and correlation.
  + often better in weak-data or prior-dominated regimes.

\vspace{0.06in}

Choice depends on amount of information per group-level predictor plus what you know about the data.

* [A General Framework for the Parametrization of Hierarchical Models, Papaspiliopoulos et al.](https://arxiv.org/pdf/0708.3797)

* [Hamiltonian Monte Carlo for Hierarchical Models, Betancourt and Girolami](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1312.0906)
